rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, lingular segments, and in lung bases on the left. A few nodules are present in the lung parenchyma; series 5 is as follows:
- In the right lung:
- In the upper lobe:
- Micronodules measuring up to 1–2 mm, no change.
- In the left lung:
- In the upper lobe:
- Subsolid nodule measuring up to 6 mm (image 185), no change.
- In the lower lobe:
- Ground-glass nodule of moderate intensity measuring up to 7 mm (image 212), no change.
No pleural effusion.
The trachea and main bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass lesions or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Aortic valve calcification. Pericardial fluid measuring up to 1.4 cm in thickness, no change. Moderate coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated; wall calcification is present. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:
Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation (spondylosis). In the T11 vertebral body, a lucent lesion measuring up to 1.4 × 1.0 × 8 mm with well-defined margins, no change compared with the earlier study dated May 12, 2025.

Upper abdomen:
Diffuse thickening of the adrenal glands measuring up to 1.1 cm, no change.
The splenic parenchyma is heterogeneous due to hypodense fluid-density lesions measuring up to 2.8 × 2.7 cm with calcification along their contour and within them (along septa), no change.
Calcification along the liver capsule in the subdiaphragmatic region measuring up to 5 mm.
rad_impression_en:
1. CT findings of a few lesions in the left lung (subsolid and ground-glass), with no change compared with the prior study. Per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations: after follow-up CT in 3–6 months confirming stability of the subsolid lesion, annual CT should be performed for 5 years. Micronodules in the right lung. No new lesions.

2. Moderate coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Moderate pericardial effusion, no change.

3. Aortic valve calcification.

4. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

5. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation (spondylosis). Lucent bone lesion (Bone-RADS 2) with stable size compared with CT dated May 12, 2025, likely degenerative-dystrophic in origin.

6. Hypodense fluid-density lesions in the splenic parenchyma with areas of calcification (likely calcified cysts); CT follow-up is not required. Diffuse thickening of the adrenal glands.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, lingular segments, and in lung bases. Gravitational atelectasis in the dorsal parts, single subpleural cysts in the dorsal part of the left lung. A few nodules are present in the lung parenchyma; series 5 is as follows:
In the right upper lobe:
- Micronodules measure 2-3 mm (image 35, 48, 72), no change.
- Calcified nodule measures 3 mm (image 84), no change.
In the left upper lobe:
- Ground-glass nodule measures 5 mm (image 185), no change.
In the left lower lobe:
- Ground-glass nodule measures 6 mm (image 212), no change.
No pleural effusion. The trachea and main bronchi are patent with a small amount of mucus.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass lesions or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe. Pericardial fluid measures up to 1.4 cm in thickness, no change. 

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated; moderate wall calcification is present. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation (spondylosis). In the T11 vertebral body, a lucent lesion measures up to 1.4 × 1.0 × 8 mm with well-defined margins (image 276), no change compared with the earlier study dated May 12, 2025.

Upper abdomen: The splenic parenchyma is heterogeneous due to hypodense fluid-density lesions measuring up to 2.8 × 2.7 cm with calcification along their contour and within them (along septa), no change, possible sequelae of granulomatous disease, CT follow-up is not required. The liver shows a capsular calcification in the right lobe superior segment, CT follow-up is not required.
val_impression_en:
1. Stable ground-glass nodule up to 6 mm in the left lung compared to previous CT dated October 10, 2025; no new pulmonary lesions detected. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: Follow-up chest CT every 2 years until 5 years after the initial discovery of the nodules.

2. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta. 

3. Moderate pericardial effusion, no change. 


DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura:  
A previously identified nodule in the left lower lobe is no longer seen. A 4 mm solid subpleural nodule in the right middle lobe (series 5, image 141), with a subtle increase in size. Linear atelectasis in the lingular segments (clinically not significant). There are solitary perifissural solid nodules up to 2 mm, polygonal in shape, likely intrapulmonary lymph nodes. Persistent areas of reticular opacity in the supradiaphragmatic portions of both lungs, likely atelectatic changes (clinically not significant). No pleural effusion. Pleuroapical pleural thickening bilaterally.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass. No lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with calcification of the wall of the descending segment. The pulmonary trunk is dilated (3.2 cm).

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Anterolisthesis of the T2 and T4 vertebrae by 3 mm. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 135 HU, T12 - 118 HU.

Included abdomen: Unremarkable.
rad_impression_en:
1. A 4 mm solid nodule in the right middle lobe with interval growth. Per Fleischner Society 2017 recommendations, consider chest CT in 12 months.

2. Complete regression of the previously identified nodule in the left lower lobe.

3. Dilated pulmonary trunk. This may be consistent with pulmonary arterial hypertension.

4. Moderate coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Thoracic aortic calcification.

6. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Anterolisthesis of the T2 and T4 vertebrae. Findings of osteopenia.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: A previously identified nodule in the left lower lobe is no longer seen. A 4 mm solid subpleural nodule in the right middle lobe (series 5, image 141), with a subtle increase in size. There are solitary perifissural solid nodules up to 2 mm, polygonal in shape, likely intrapulmonary lymph nodes, require no follow up. Thickening of the interlobular interstitium at the lung bases, unchanged. In the lower lobes, peribronchial areas have areas of ground glass, unchanged. Linear atelectasis and small bronchiectasis in the lingular segments (clinically insignificant). No pleural effusion. Pleuroapical scarring bilaterally.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass. No lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild calcification of the wall of the descending segment. The pulmonary trunk is dilated (3.9 cm).

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Degenerative changes of the shoulder joints. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 135 HU, T12 - 118 HU. Hemangioma in the T12 vertebral body requires no follow up.

Included abdomen: Unremarkable.
val_impression_en:
1. A 4 mm solid nodule in the right middle lobe with interval growth. Per Fleischner Society 2017 recommendations, consider chest CT in 12 months.
Complete regression of the previously identified nodule in the left lower lobe.

2. Thickening of the interlobular interstitium and areas of ground glass at the lung bases, unchanged, may be associated with heart disease or interstitial changes.

3. Dilated pulmonary trunk (3.9 cm). This may be consistent with pulmonary arterial hypertension.

4. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Thoracic aortic calcification.

6. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Degenerative changes of the shoulder joints. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteoporosis. Hemangioma in the T12 vertebral body requires no follow up.

rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Hypoventilatory changes in the lower basal portions of the lungs.

In the subpleural portions of the lungs, areas of paraseptal emphysema persist with areas of fibrosis (more pronounced in the upper lobes of the lungs, honeycombing pattern), visually without interval change, with single air bullae (largest along the right interlobar fissure up to 1.5 × 1.4 cm, without interval change).

In the lung parenchyma, single solid nodules are present, in series 5 as follows:
In the right upper lobe:
* Nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 93), without interval change.
In the right lower lobe:
* Nodule measuring up to 7 mm (image 121), without interval change.
In the left lower lobe:
* Nodule measuring up to 1.1 × 0.9 cm with lobulated margins (image 74), adjacent to the interlobar fissure, not previously identified.

No pleural effusion. In the posterior basal portions, an area of costal pleural thickening persists with numerous large calcifications within it; an area of calcification of the right interlobar fissure, visually without interval change.

The trachea and main bronchi are patent, with focal wall calcifications. The transverse tracheal lumen is mildly narrowed and the anteroposterior dimension is widened (saber-sheath trachea), visually without interval change.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass. No lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Multiple healing rib fractures (more on the left). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with small osteophytes. Decreased height and wedge deformity of the T7 vertebral body up to 33% without spinal canal narrowing at this level. Decreased height of the C5-C7 vertebral bodies up to 45%. Mild decreased density (+145 HU) of the T12 vertebral bone tissue, findings of osteopenia.

Included upper abdomen: Small calcifications partially included within the scan field at the level of the upper and middle calyx groups of the right kidney up to 3 mm.
rad_impression_en:
1. Solid nodule measuring 1.1 × 0.9 cm in the left lower lobe, not previously identified. Per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, short-interval CT follow-up (in 3 months) is recommended, and consider positron emission tomography CT and/or tissue sampling. Other single solid lung nodules show no interval change.

2. CT findings of pulmonary emphysema with severe fibrotic changes, and saber-sheath trachea, findings of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Right pleural thickening with areas of calcification, which may correspond to postinflammatory changes, visually without interval change.

3. Moderate coronary artery calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Wedge deformity of the T7 and C5-C7 vertebral bodies (without findings of significant spinal canal narrowing at this level) which may be a sign of osteoporosis, and CT findings of osteopenia at T12. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with small osteophytes. Multiple healing rib fractures bilaterally.

7. Calcifications partially included in the region of the right renal calyces (calculi versus vascular calcifications or calcifications of sinus elements). Given partial visualization, reliable assessment is limited.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Coarse peripheral reticular interstitial opacities with some honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis, most pronounced in the upper lobes, with mild interval worsening compared to March 24, 2023. Areas of paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema. 
In the lung parenchyma, a few solid nodules are present, in series 5 as follows:
In the right upper lobe:
* Nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 93), without interval change.
In the right lower lobe:
* Nodule measuring up to 7 mm (image 121), without interval change.
In the left lower lobe:
* Nodule measuring up to 1.1 × 0.9 cm with lobulated margins (image 74), adjacent to the interlobar fissure, not previously identified.
No pleural effusion. In the posterior basal portions, an area of costal pleural thickening persists with numerous large calcifications; an area of calcification of the right interlobar fissure, without interval change.
The trachea and main bronchi are patent, with focal wall calcifications. The transverse tracheal lumen is mildly narrowed and the anteroposterior dimension is widened (saber-sheath trachea), visually without interval change. The walls of large and segmental bronchi are thickened.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass. No lymphadenopathy. Dilated, thin-walled esophagus.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Mitral valve calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with moderate wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Multiple healing rib fractures. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine and shoulder joints, thoracic scoliosis. Severe degenerative changes of the cervical spine. Decreased height and wedge deformity of the T7 vertebral body up to 33% without spinal canal narrowing at this level. Mild decreased density (+145 HU) of the T12 vertebral bone tissue, findings of osteopenia.

Included upper abdomen: Small calculi of the right kidney up to 3 mm, no hydronephrosis.
val_impression_en:
1. Solid nodule measuring 1.1 × 0.9 cm in the left lower lobe, not previously identified. Per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, short-interval CT follow-up (in 3 months) is recommended, and consider positron emission tomography CT and/or tissue sampling. Other solid nodules show no interval change.

2. Pulmonary emphysema and saber-sheath trachea (findings of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease), interstitial fibrotic changes without  basal predominance. Right pleural thickening with calcification, which may correspond to postinflammatory changes, without interval change.

3. Moderate coronary artery calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Mitral valve calcification. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Wedge deformity of the T7 vertebral body, which may be a sign of osteoporosis. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine and shoulder joints, thoracic scoliosis. Severe degenerative changes of the cervical spine. Multiple healing rib fractures bilaterally.

5. Small calculi of the right kidney up to 3 mm, no hydronephrosis.

rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules on series 5 are as follows:  
Right lower lobe:  
- Solid subpleural nodule measuring 4 mm on image 137 (no change).  
In both lungs, micronodules measuring up to 2–3 mm (no change). In both lungs, mild paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification is present in the left anterior descending artery, qualitatively mild.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated; mild wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Bilateral mammoplasty.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
rad_impression_en:
1. Solid nodule in the right lower lobe and micronodules in both lungs, stable compared with March 26, 2025; per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, no additional CT follow-up is recommended. Mild paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema in both lungs.

2. Coronary calcification, qualitatively mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

3. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Bilateral mammoplasty.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules on series 5 are as follows.
Right upper lobe:
- Ground-glass nodule measures 3 mm on image 55 (no change).
Right lower lobe:
- Solid subpleural nodule measures 4 mm on image 137 (no change).
In both lungs, micronodules measure up to 2–3 mm (no change). In the right lower lobe there is a paravertebral area of osteophyte-induced fibrosis. Mild paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification is present in the left anterior descending artery, qualitatively assessed as mild.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated; mild wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Bilateral breast implants.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
val_impression_en:
1. A few nodules in both lungs, stable compared with March 26, 2025; per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, no additional CT follow-up is recommended.

2. Mild paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema in both lungs. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening.

3. Coronary calcification is present in the left anterior descending artery, qualitatively mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura:
Linear atelectatic bands in the left basal segments.
Mixed emphysema in the lungs, with air bullae measuring up to 1.5 cm.
In the right upper lobe, there is a cavitary lesion with overall dimensions of about 2.7 × 2.1 cm (series 5, image 279), with a solid component measuring about 1.9 × 1.0 cm and a linear hyperdense inclusion up to 4 mm. The contours of the above-described lesion are irregular, with pleural tags to the costal pleura and, less prominently, to the mediastinal pleura. Adjacent subsegmental bronchi of segment 3 are retracted toward the lesion, and their walls are thickened.
A few perifissural micronodules bilaterally.
Otherwise: No nodules requiring CT follow-up are identified in the lungs.
No pleural effusion.
Trachea and central bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:
No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart:
Cardiac chambers are enlarged.
Aortic valve calcification.
Fluid in the pericardial cavity measures up to 1.1 cm in thickness.
Mild coronary artery calcification in the left anterior descending and circumflex arteries. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels:
Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcification of the wall is present.
Main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.7 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:
Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased bone density in the L1 vertebral body to +149 HU. Schmorl's node of the superior endplate of L2 measuring up to 1.1 cm.

Upper abdomen:
A few hypodense lesions in the liver parenchyma, within the imaged field, with well-defined smooth contours and fluid density, measuring up to 1.1 × 1.0 cm.
Lymph node in the lesser omentum is enlarged to 1.1 cm in short axis (series 4, image 253), and along the hepatoduodenal ligament to 1.1 cm in short axis (image 249).

Thyroid gland:
Asymmetric, left greater than right. In the left lobe, there is a heterogeneous nodular lesion measuring up to 3.4 × 2.4 cm with hypodense areas.
rad_impression_en:
1. CT findings of a cavitary lesion in the right upper lobe, which, in correlation with clinical, laboratory, and historical data, may represent an aspergilloma. No other nodules requiring CT follow-up are identified in the lungs.
Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer.
Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening.
Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has a history of lung cancer, known pulmonary nodules or lung mass, receives routine chest CTs/screening, or is receiving palliative care.

2. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.7 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

3. Mild coronary artery calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Small pericardial effusion.

7. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased density of the L1 vertebral body. Schmorl's node at L2.

8. Hypodense liver lesions: in low-risk patients (correlate with clinical history), according to "Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee," 2017, CT follow-up is not required.

9. Abdominal lymphadenopathy.

10. Nodular changes of the thyroid gland: according to "Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the ACR Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee," 2014, thyroid ultrasound is recommended.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Diffuse multiple pulmonary cysts with thin walls and upper lobe predominance. In the right upper lobe, there is a cavity with bronchial connection, irregular wall, solid mural component, and metal foreign body. Overall dimensions are about 2.7 × 2.1 cm (series 5, image 279). A solid component measures 1.9 × 1.0 cm, and a foreign body measures up to 4 mm (3000 HU). There are multiple strands to the costal pleura and to the mediastinal pleura. Adjacent subsegmental bronchi are retracted toward the lesion, and their walls are thickened, with contents in their lumen. Two calcified nodules measure up to 4 mm in both lungs (image 127 on the right and image 78 on the left), healed granulomas, require no follow-up. Mild interstitial changes in lung bases (clinically insignificant). No pleural effusion. Trachea and central bronchi are patent. Wall thickening and secretion of the segmental and subsegmental bronchi.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: Cardiac chambers are enlarged. Aortic valve calcification. Fluid in the pericardial cavity measures up to 1.1 cm in thickness. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated (3.9 cm), with atherosclerotic plaques. Main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.7 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with ossification of the anterior longitudinal ligament.

Upper abdomen: Multiple hypodense lesions in the liver parenchyma with well-defined smooth contours and fluid density, measuring up to 9 mm. Lymph node in the lesser omentum is enlarged to 1.1 cm in short axis (series 4, image 253), and along the hepatoduodenal ligament to 1.1 cm in short axis (image 249).

Thyroid gland: Asymmetric, left lobe is enlarged, heterogeneous with nodular lesions.
val_impression_en:
1. The right upper lobe cavity with bronchial connection, irregular wall, solid mural component, and metal foreign body may represent a pulmonary mycetoma (aspergilloma) and requires follow-up.

2. Diffuse cystic lung disease with upper lobe predominance. According to Managing Incidental Findings on Thoracic CT: Lung Findings. A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2021, consider pulmonary Langerhans cell histiocytosis.

3. Wall thickening and secretion of the segmental and subsegmental bronchi may represent bronchitis.

4. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.7 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

5. Moderate coronary artery calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Aortic valve calcification. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Small-volume pericardial effusion.

7. Hypodense liver lesions. ACR White Paper recommendations (Gore, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1429-1437) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of having a malignant hepatic lesion, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of having a malignant hepatic lesion, follow-up MRI in 3 to 6 months is recommended for further evaluation.

8. Abdominal lymphadenopathy.

9. Heterogeneous and enlarged left thyroid lobe. ACR White Paper recommendations (Hoang, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2015;12:143-150) suggest the following: Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
